title: The Rich Man and Lazarus at the Gate
subtitle: A man feasting in purple, a beggar full of sores lying at his door

stillness: Be still, for God sees the heart.
If we always thought of God present, seeing all things, and avenging all things,
we would scarcely, or never, sin.
So even when you are alone, turn from evil and do good.
gospel: There was a certain rich man,
who was clothed in purple and fine linen,
And there was a certain beggar, named Lazarus,
who lay at his gate, full of sores,
desiring to be filled with the crumbs that fell from the rich man's table,
and no one gave to him.
And the dogs came and licked his sores.
behold: Attend to each thing as if you were present.
Behold this rich man — rich not so much in the having of wealth as in the love of it.
See the superfluity of his senses, the lust of the eyes.
He is clothed in purple above, for empty glory,
and in white linen against his flesh, for softness:
behold the excess of his dress, and the pride of life.
He feasts every day, serving his gluttony more than his nature:
behold the excess of his table, and the lust of the flesh.
Now behold the beggar lying at his gate, full of sores,
set there that the hardness of the rich man might appear the more,
as he went out and came in and saw him with his own eyes.
And the dogs came and licked his sores —
the dogs were more courteous to the poor man than their master.
consider: The rich man's name is passed over in silence, but the poor man's name is spoken.
For he was not known to God by approbation; as the Saviour says to the reprobate: I never knew you.
Unknown to God, he is marked by no name, for he had sought his name from the world.
But Lazarus was known to God by approbation, as it was said to Moses: I have known thee by name.
The names of the good are written in the book of God, the names of the wicked in the book of the devil.
The rich are famous in the world by their names, but the poor lie hidden and have no name there.
God looks upon these near at hand, and holds those off afar.
The rich have their names on the summit of the world; the poor have their names in the hall of God.
Chrysostom cries: Most wretched of men, you see the death of your own body lying before your gate, and you have no pity!
If you do not weigh God's commandments, at least pity your own condition, and fear lest you yourself become such.
He longed to be filled — not with precious things, but with the crumbs that fell from the table.
As if saying to the rich man: the crumbs of your table suffice me, which you throw away.
Give then your alms from your surplus; make gain from what is no loss to you.
Here too great hardness appears: he let perish what might have lifted the poor man up.
And no one gave to him, for such a master, such a household — they were conformed to him in hardness of mind.
Sickness has some solace, if it has wealth; but if poverty be added to the greatness of the sickness, the infirmity is doubled.
How great the pain in this man, who amid so many wounds remembered not the pain of his blows, but his hunger.
prayer: since I am utterly poor in virtues, full of the sores of my sins,
a beggar laid before the gate of your mercy,
longing to be filled with the crumbs of good examples that fall from the table of the rich, your sons,
who are clothed in the purple of the passions and feast sumptuously each day on the dishes of the virtues:
let your dogs, your teachers, come and lick the wounds of my sins,
that, dying to the world, I may be carried by the Angels into the bosom of heavenly contemplation.
Let me hear Moses and the Prophets, turning from evil and doing good,
that, rising from dead works, I may hope to have eternal life.

latinJewel.latin: Divitibus sunt nomina in culmine mundi, sed pauperibus sunt nomina in aula Dei.
latinJewel.english: The rich have their names on the summit of the world, but the poor have their names in the hall of God.
